summary_text:
S:
The patient reports a pop in his right knee during a soccer game three days ago, followed by swelling. He states the swelling has decreased somewhat, but the knee feels limited in its range of motion and is not feeling normal. He reports pain between 6 and 7 on a scale of 1 to 10. He has been taking ibuprofen regularly to relieve the pain. He can bend his leg but reports pain at the end of the range of motion and difficulty straightening it fully, requiring assistance. He can bear weight on it but feels it is loose. The patient reports neurovascularly his lower extremities are intact and has a muscle strength of 5/5.

O:
A significant effusion is noted in the right knee with mild ecchymosis. Passive range of motion reveals a limitation of terminal extension to 115 degrees and a limitation of terminal flexion to 20-30 degrees secondary to pain. A positive Lachman exam is performed. The patient demonstrates a marked limp upon ambulation.

A:
Based on the examination, the patient's right knee injury is highly suggestive of an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear. An MRI is ordered to evaluate the integrity of the ACL. Management will initially focus on stabilizing the knee with a knee immobilizer and crutches to minimize stress.

P:
Order an MRI of the right knee. Prescribe meloxicam 50 mg daily for pain and inflammation. Advise the patient to ice the knee as needed. Instruct the patient to wear a knee immobilizer and use crutches for weight-bearing. Recommend slowing down jogging until further evaluation. Schedule follow-up to discuss MRI results and determine the next steps in treatment.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right knee injury.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Scott Miller is a pleasant 36-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right knee injury. The injury occurred 3 days ago, when he was playing pickup soccer games and felt a pop in his right knee. He reports swelling at the time of the injury, which has improved. The patient states that his knee is not as functional or moving as well as it used to be. He rates his pain level as a 6 to 7 out of 10 currently and a 4 to 5 out of 10 when the swelling decreases. The patient has been taking ibuprofen on a regular basis, which provides some relief. He states that he is able to bend his knee with pain towards the end of this motion; however, when he goes to straighten his knee, he feels like he has to help it straight. The patient adds that he is able to bear weight on his right knee, however it feels loose and does not feel normal.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient works at a desk.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right knee pain.

PHYSICAL EXAM

GAIT: Marked limp in the right leg upon ambulation.
MSK: Examination of the right knee: Significant effusion. Slight ecchymosis. Pain with palpation at the medial joint line. Difficulty with passive ROM. Lacking a few degrees of terminal extension to 115 degrees. Lacking 20-30 degrees of terminal flexion secondary to pain. Positive Lachman's test. Muscle strength is 5/5. Neurovascular intact distally.

ASSESSMENT

Right knee pain, possible anterior cruciate ligament tear.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have recommended that we obtain an MRI of the right knee to evaluate the integrity of the ACL. I have prescribed the patient meloxicam 15 mg a day to reduce swelling and pain. We discussed that the patient should utilize ice and activity modification to reduce swelling and pain. I recommended that he wears a knee immobilizer and use crutches when he is ambulating. He will avoid jogging until we have the results of his MRI.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me once the MRI results are available for review and further discussion.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports neurovascularly his lower extremities are intact and has a muscle strength of 5/5.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Neurovascular status and muscle strength are typically clinician-assessed objective exam findings; phrasing as patient-reported is atypical and could mislead a reader about how these findings were obtained.

section_miscategorizations.text: An MRI is ordered to evaluate the integrity of the ACL.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Ordering imaging is a management action and belongs in the Plan, not in the Assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: Management will initially focus on stabilizing the knee with a knee immobilizer and crutches to minimize stress.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Description of intended management (knee immobilizer and crutches) is part of the treatment plan rather than diagnostic assessment.
